rationale: The page contains detailed information regarding the India-Pakistan Indus Waters Treaty (IWT), including recent developments, treaty provisions, dispute resolution mechanisms, and international water-sharing principles. This is high-quality content for current affairs questions.
cleanedText:
o New Maritime Silk Road ("Road"): Maritime route connecting China to Southeast Asia, Gulf countries, East/North Africa, and Europe.
• Recently expanded: China and Colombia signed BRI cooperation pact.
3.2. INDIA-PAKISTAN
Why in the News?
India announced the Indus Waters Treaty (IWT) of 1960 with Pakistan will be held ‘in abeyance’ with immediate effect, until Pakistan stops its support for cross-border terrorism, after recent terror attack.
More on the news
• No Treaty Provision for Abeyance: Neither IWT nor the Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties (VCLT) 1969 provides 'abeyance' as grounds for halting treaty obligations.
• No Unilateral Suspension Allowed: IWT lacks provisions for unilateral suspension.
About Indus Waters Treaty (IWT)
• Genesis: Signed between India and Pakistan and brokered by the World Bank in 1960.
• Objective: Fixed and delimited the rights concerning the use of the waters of the Indus River system.
• Provisions for water usage rights:
o India: unrestricted use of Ravi, Beas, and Sutlej.
o Pakistan: Indus, Jhelum, and Chenab.
o Can be used by India with for irrigation, transport, etc.
• Implementation: Created Permanent Indus Commission to maintain a channel for communication; meet at least once every year.
• Indus Waters Treaty Dispute Resolution Process
o Permanent Indus Commission: Resolves issues related to treaty implementation.
o Neutral Expert: Appointed by the World Bank to address unresolved water-sharing differences and provide a binding decision.
o Court of Arbitration (CoA): If the neutral expert fails, the dispute is referred to the CoA, a seven-member tribunal that decides by majority vote.
• Neutral Experts: Is inspecting Kishenganga and Ratle Hydroelectric Projects.
o Kishenganga HEP: Run-of-the-river project diverting water from Kishenganga to Jhelum in J&K.
o Ratle HEP: Located on Chenab River in Kishtwar, J&K.
Indus Water Treaty Division of Rivers
• Western Rivers (Pakistan's control): Indus, Jhelum, Chenab.
• Eastern Rivers (India's control): Sutlej, Beas, Ravi.
International principles on international water sharing
• Helsinki Rules, 1966: Adopted by the International Law Association and regulating “the use of water of an international drainage basin”.
• Helsinki Convention, 1992: Provides a legal framework for preventing and controlling water pollution across national borders.
• UN Watercourses Convention, 1997: United Nations Convention on the Law of the Non-Navigational Uses of International Watercourses (UN Watercourses Convention) codified international law on trans-boundary water resources.
o India, China and Pakistan have not signed.
